title: 2025 12 10 FEC
displayTitle: 2025-12-10 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Financial Markets Conduct Amendment Bill
description: Wednesday 10 December 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Financial Markets Conduct Amendment Bill - Amendment Paper 446 Financial Services Federation Lyn McMorran, Executive Director Investor Group on Climate Change Francesca Muskovic, Executive Director – Policy, Investor Group on Climate Change NZX Limited Kristin Brandon, Head of Policy and Regulatory Affairs Simon Beattie, General Manager Corporate Affairs & Sustainability Responsible Investment Association Australasia (RIAA) Dean Hegarty, Co-CEO Tower Limited Paul Johnston, Chief Executive Officer McGuinness Institute Wendy McGuinness, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Financial Markets Conduct Amendment Bill. Financial Markets Conduct Amendment Bill - Amendment Paper 446. Financial Services Federation

chunks.text: ... Conduct Amendment Bill. And so, uh, we've got, uh, Lynne McMorrin here from Financial Services Federation. Lynne, as you know, we're under a bit of time pressure, but we're in your hands, so welcome. Thank you. Good afternoon, and thank you for the opportunity for the Financial Services Federation to speak to our submission on this bill. As you can see from our submission, the FSF is the industry body representing the specialist lending sector operating in New Zealand, and our members have over 1.5 million New Zealand consumers and businesses who choose to be their

customers. As we said in our written submission, the FSF is supportive of the intent of the amendments to climate-related disclosure obligations as outlined in the amendment paper, but we believe that they could go further. At present, only two of our 100 members are required to meet climate-related disclosure obligations as they currently stand.

The proposed changes to the definitions the amendment will make will not change that, and both entities will continue to be subject to the requirement to make climate-related disclosures. Whilst the remainder of our members do not have to make their own disclosures, it's often the case that they have to provide some form of disclosure to other entities with whom they interact.

For example, their funding partners such as the large banks as part of their partners' disclosure obligations. The cost to a business of being compliant with their climate-related disclosure obligations has been significant, and our listed issuing reporting member reports that the data quality is dubious due to subjective interpretation and assumptions that have to be made.

The FSF questions whether the imposition of the climate-related disclosure regime has made any appreciable dif- difference to... Has done has created significant cost to business and a lucrative sideline for professional advisors in assisting reporting entities to meet their obligations. This results in highly qualified opinions providing meaningless assurance. The climate-related disclosure regime is a strong disincentive to companies becoming listed issuers, and the New Zealand Stock Exchange and the in- investing public suffers as a result.

This relax in a- uh, results in a lack of diversity and reduces the options for businesses to raise capital to grow and be more productive and to contribute more to the New Zealand economy. The FSF believes that the climate-related disclosure regime could usefully b- be eliminated entirely, and we do not believe that in doing so the emissions emanating from reporting entities would appreciably change from where they are now. Failing that, however, the FSF strongly suggests that committee consider further raising the reporting threshold beyond the pro-

proposed one billion to at least two billion, if not five. That's essentially our submission and I'm happy to take any questions. Okay. Thanks, Lynne. I've got a question here from Marina Williams. Hi, Lynne. Um, good- Hi.... to see you and thank you for coming along. Um- Thank you. Good to see you. A- a quick question about, um, you've described the problem with, um, dubious decisions being made along the way on these reporting, um, decisions that get made within a business.

Um, do you agree with, um, some submitters, say the submission of Chapman Tripp which suggests a purpose clause to make this clearer and to address the problem that you're highlighting there? Oh, without a doubt. I, I think, yeah, raising the threshold and making it clearer as to what the obligations actually are. They're very opaque. I think there was, there was an entire industry sort of developed around helping entities to meet their climate reporting, uh, disclosure obligations because it was so unclear as to what

exactly they were supposed to be reporting on. Um, and I think, um, you know, I've sat in on a number of meetings around what was required and came away none the wiser from most of them, to be fair. Um, and I think, you know, it, it became an exercise in just ticking boxes and doing what had to, um, you know, being seen to be doing what needed to be done but really not actually contributing anything worthwhile to the discussion.

Okay. Okay. Thank you. Any other questions? Uh, Dr. Wilson? Yes. Thank you, Lynne. Um, can you explain to us what reasoning you have behind, uh, raising the threshold to two to five billion? What's- Well, I think, uh-... the rationale? ... particularly for... Well, for the listed issuers, uh, so as I say, we have two members who currently have to disclose.

Um, one is a listed issuer and one is a building society. Um, the listed issuer, uh, obviously, m- um, is beyond the threshold of the 60 million as it currently stands, and the building society s- is just above that one billion threshold. Um, it is a really strong disincentive for anybody to list on the, on the Stock Exchange in New Zealand if they have to, uh, put in place the climate-related disclosure obligations.

Um, and, you know, a $1 billion company is not an enormous-sized company when you consider, you know, large banks, large insurers, larger, larger entities. Um, and I think that's a, a, you know, as a disincentive for becoming a listed issuer, I think that does New Zealand a disservice in a lot of ways. Um, it's sort of well-recognized that the New Zealand Stock Exchange, um, doesn't, um, have a broad range of listings and that doesn't then provide a diversified, um, investment range of options. Um, and I think that, you know,

by raising the threshold, that might encourage more people to seek capital, uh, capital raising through l- becoming a listed entity. Um, but climate-related disclosure is definitely a, a dis- a, a strong incentive to doing so. Okay. No, loud and clear. No, thank you, uh, Lynne, for your, um, supplementary submission and appearing before the committee this afternoon.
You're welcome. Thank you for the time. Thank you. Okay, members, that takes us to the Investor Group on Climate Change, also online.

Okay. We've got Frankie Muskovic here. Hello. Good, good afternoon. Good afternoon. Thanks for having me. I can assume you can hear me. Yes, we can. No, yeah, we've got 10 minutes or so. Um, we're in your hands. Great. Well, kia ora. I'm Frankie Muskovic from the Investor Group on Climate Change. Our membership comprises institutional investors with interests in Australia and New Zealand, focused on the impact of climate change on the retirement savings of their beneficiaries and client investment outcomes.

Uh, we represent investors with total funds under management of over 5 trillion in Australia and New Zealand, and over 40 trillion around the world.At a time when climate risks are increasingly manifesting as severe economic damage, the proposal to significantly shrink coverage of Aotearoa New Zealand's climate-related disclosures regime and remove applicability to manage investment scheme managers altogether will undermine investor confidence in New Zealand businesses.

The success of your government's economic growth agenda will depend on the country's ability to attract global investment in what is an increasingly competitive landscape. Far from attracting capital, this move will undermine the government's agenda by removing a critical transparency measure.

And a significant reduction in the number of reporting entities will fragment the landscape and hinder informed investment decisions. I would note that we're, we're living in a context where many jurisdictions have introduced mandatory climate reporting, including Australia, the European Union, the UK, California. And once a leader, New Zealand risks falling behind countries like China, Chile, and Singapore, some of the 40 jurisdictions looking to adopt the International Sustainability Standards Board standards for mandatory climate reporting.

Raising the reporting threshold for listed equity and debt issuers from 60 million to one billion will exclude a substantial proportion of the economy from reporting obligations. That removes critical transparency for investors on how a wide range of companies are managing financially material climate risks. We note the consultation by MB only contemplated a maximum increase to the threshold of 550 million, and it's unclear on what evidence and rationale the decision to almost double that proposed maximum is based. Indeed, we understand there was relatively little support for

raising the threshold to even the 550 million upper limit from respondents to that consultation. The wholesale removal of managed investment scheme managers from the regime is also troubling. Those entities manage approximately 230 billion in assets, capital that is exposed to climate-related risks and opportunities.

And preempting the refinements that XRB indicated were under consideration to make the regime more suitable for investors and smaller entities is a radical step, and it weakens stewardship and accountability across the financial system. Uh, we urge the government to reconsider these changes and maintain a strong, credible climate disclosure regime.

Investors need transparency, consistency, and accountability. And these changes come at a time when reporting entities have only just recently built capacity to meet the current requirements. I would note that in the early phases of introducing, uh, reforms like this, it takes a little while for industry, uh, and, you know, companies to get their heads wrapped around this, and flip-flopping and going the other way is only gonna add to that uncertainty, uh, in the longer term. So again, once again, we urge the government to reject these changes and instead, uh, move

ahead with the process that XRB was already in train to deliver in terms of refinements to the scheme. I'm really happy to take any questions. Thanks. Hmm. Okay. Thank you very much. We're gonna go to, uh, Dr. Megan Woods. (clears throat) Thank you very much for your submission. Um, (clears throat) I'm just interested in what you raise around the, the policy uncertainty and how this will misalign us with a number of other companies that we are looking really to compete for capital, uh, with an investment incoming capital.

But I'm interested in the, the trading aspect that you note in your submission, that actually 80% of our trading partners will have disclosure regimes, and, um, whether you, whether you think there'll be any potential impacts here. Yeah. That's right. And so, um, n- none of us, even though we're physically islands, um, we're, we're globally so interconnected when it comes to the investment landscape.

And if you consider New Zealand's major trading partners, the vast majority of them either have mandatory related, uh, climate disclosures in place, uh, or are considering it. And so, you know, w- whilst the view might be, um, from some companies that this is an onerous requirement, uh, r- removing or radically raising that threshold is gonna put New Zealand businesses at a strong disadvantage when it comes to attracting that global investment.

There are strong regimes in, and that have been in place for some time, uh, in the EU, the UK. Uh, Australia's regime is rolling out now. And I think investors will look at what's happening in New Zealand and wonder, "If there's a change in government in a couple of years' time, am I going to see yet another reversal?" That kind of uncertainty does not (laughs) does not, uh, provide an attractive investment landscape for investors. And countries like New Zealand and Australia rely on global investment. So this is not just a decision that can be

taken, uh, you know, with a view of domestic businesses only. You have to consider the views, uh, and, and frankly the requirements of global investors that will be looking at your market. Thank you. Thanks, Dr. Woods. I'm gonna go to Erina Williams. Thanks, Frankie. Um, I wonder if you could step us through the decision-making for a major investor. Say, um, you're a fund manager based out of Australia. You've got, um, some different sorts of offerings including, like, a green fund, but another fund that, um, is aimed at, um, your average consumer.

What kind of information can you get about New Zealand companies now versus New Zealand companies under the regime change, and how is that impacting your investment decisions, um, if, you know, any other DD you have to do is going to take, take, you know, more work and impact on the level of investment that you can make?

Well, I think part of the reason that having a disclosure regime that covers, you know, at least a significant portion of companies, you know, and, and listed equities and things like that- Mm-hmm.... um, is that it gives investors a better overall sense of how investable, um, you know, a, a market is.

And so by raising the, I guess, the proposal to raise this threshold so significantly means you're capturing such a small proportion, um, of New Zealand businesses, uh, that it means that when an investor's got, you know, disclosures, um, that they might have from, I don't know, companies in Australia or, or the EU that have been, you know, doing these disclosures for a number of years, I think we'd all acknowledge, right, the initial, uh, rollout of these, um, reforms takes a while to sort through teething issues. Uh, you've gotta, you've gotta work

through, you know, the, the level of confidence around assurance and things like that. But that does smooth out over time. So if I'm a business and I'm looking at investments in real assets or infrastructure and I know that those assets are subject to, uh, physical risks from increasing, uh, extreme weather events, uh, you know, heat and things like that, and I've got a company that has done the work to map its risks, uh, tell me how they're addressing those risks, that is a less risky investment, uh, to an investor, where that

information is not so easily visible. And it's, it basically comes down to, uh, the, the company's approach to risk management. It's a core issue. And if companies aren't doing this and, and providing disclosures in a public way on climate, which is an increasingly significant, uh, risk for companies, then it's going to... You know, if all other things are equal, investors will choose an investment in a company that has looked at those risks and is- Mm-hmm.

... actively managing them. Okay. Okay. Dr. David Wilson, you got a quick one? Thank you. Um, Ms. Maskovic, uh, could you tell us, um, do you think that, um, concern, public concern and social license and competitive environments are not enough in and of themselves for companies to outline their cre- you know, their climate responsibilities and report?

I think it's important, uh, for companies to, to engage with community in, uh, particularly in the rollout of new infrastructure. It's an and, not an or. Um, I think we have to be conscious of the different audiences, um, you know, for, for different i- uh, initiatives. And, you know, climate-related financial disclosures, uh, have a variety of purposes.

Um, I'm obviously speaking from an investor perspective, and it's a crucial input to decision-making, um, but, but disclosures can also be useful, uh, to, to consumers, uh, or s- key stakeholders that want to understand how businesses are looking at those risks and managing them. So- I-... I, I always consider it an and, not an or.

Okay. Thank- thank you. Thank you, Frankie. Thank you, Dr. Wilson. Um, thank you for your supplementary submission and appearing before us, uh, this afternoon. Thanks. Thanks for having me. Thank you. Okay. That brings us to a live appearance, uh, of NZX Limited. Um, we've got Kristen, K- Kirsten... Sorry. We have got Kristen, not Kirsten, Kristen Brandon, and we've got Simon Beattie, um, from NZX Limited. And as they go to the table, can I note, uh, Mr. Beattie, that next month marks 40 years since we started secondary school together?

(laughs) (laughs) And, and I have to say that I'm holding up remarkably well. (laughs) (laughs) You may be Mr. Chair, but I'm still scarred. (laughs) (laughs) Okay. Well, let's keep to topic today, please. We're under a time- No, (laughs) (laughs) (laughs) We are under a tight time pressure. Back to the bill, please.

Back to the bill. (laughs) (laughs) Back to the bill. Okay. No, welcome, um, uh, to you both, and we look forward to your submission. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and for that warm welcome, which is quite hard to follow. Um, I'm Kristen Brandon. I'm the head of policy at NZX and also chair of the NZX Corporate Governance Institute. I'll just let Simon introduce himself.

Uh, thank you, Kristen. Uh, good afternoon, uh, Mr. Chair and members of the committee. I'm Simon Beattie, Head of Corporate Affairs and Sustainability at NZX. Um, we'd like to thank you and the committee members for letting us present this submission to you today. We are here as, um, we were introduced to represent NZX, which is New Zealand's exchange.

As a member of the Stock, Sustainable Stock Exchange Initiative and the Sustainable Business Council, NZX supports climate reporting and a climate-resilient future for New Zealand. We're here today to support the proposals contained in the amendment paper before the Select Committee, which we consider to be practical and sensible amendments that have been warmly welcomed by the listed market.

We'll now briefly outline the key points in our submission and we would then be happy to take questions. I'm gonna hand over to Simon. Thank you. Thanks, Kristen. First of all, NZX supports the amendment paper's proposed increases to the CRD reporting thresholds to $1 billion, which will create more proportionate settings. These changes will allow smaller issuers or companies listed on the exchange to focus on climate change mitigation, transition, and adaptation, rather than reporting and compliance. The costs of climate

reporting are not strongly correlated with issuer size and are not scalable. Companies have told us that the New Zealand climate reporting disclosure settings have been prohibitive to listing. Mm-hmm. Likewise, listed companies have told NZX the settings, costs, and requirements could cause them to delist.The report Lessons From The Line published by the Institute of Directors and KPMG interviewed leading directors on this aspect, who characterized the qua- the costs as, quote, "Eye-wateringly high, horrendous, and hard to

justify." For example, Turner's climate reporting costs have been publicly reported as more than a million dollars. The proposed changes to the thresholds, we believe are balanced, and support listed companies' ability to grow, invest, and create more and better-paying jobs for New Zealanders. The threshold changes outlined in the amendment paper will provide New Zealand investors greater access to investable product, including green and sustainable products, like green bonds, that may support New Zealand's transition to net zero greenhouse gas emissions by

2050. NZX also strongly supports the proposals to create a proportionate liability framework for directors. These changes create settings that should both reduce costs relating to insurance, legal advice, and assurance and encourage CREs to report further information related to climate risks and opportunities for their business.

These changes remove a barrier to listing for, for New Zealand companies. We expect the flow-on effects of these changes to include broadening of the director candidate pool in New Zealand and encouraging more diversity on boards, including in relation to first-time directors. We consider that the amended liability framework re- will result in better corporate governance and climate reporting outcomes for New Zealand.

And we're now happy to take any questions. Okay. Thanks, uh, Kristin. Right. We're gonna go to Arena Williams. Thank you. And thanks, both, for, um, being able to have, uh, submit to us today in person. Um, just wanna ask about, uh, something you said. Um, you know, whatever you think about these changes, we do want to con- ke- to keep seeing businesses invest in that adaptation and risk mitigation part of what we hope they are doing.

Um, with your submission about changing directors' liabilities, that seems to be one area where you could continue to drive more business behavior to make active decision-making decisions around the governance table to prioritize that. Um, are we getting out of step with directors' obligations in Australia if we, uh, walk back some of those directors' oversight functions that are not directly related to the reporting but are related to the mitigation, risk adaptation part of their work?

I'll, I'll just- You can. (laughs) Okay. I'll, I'll just speak on it at a high level. What we've seen, and the experience of what we've seen and what we've heard from, from, uh, listed issuers is because of the deemed director settings at, at the moment, in which directors are criminally liable for a company's statements and their forward-looking statements, that the, the, the, the risk has focused on directors and not on the business around reducing climate risk and the focus on mitigation and adaptation and, and the

transition, and the effort's gone into protecting directors and organizations because of where the settings lie. What we would like to see is for those settings to change so we see more action in regards to the, to the, the things we're talking about, which is reducing company emissions and reducing, um, New Zealand's emissions overall.

H- how do you think, um, and directors would be better incentivized to prioritize that, to put it at the top of the agenda if we, you know, go ahead with the policy change which we don't necessarily agree with. But if you are undertaking this change, how do we keep that on the agenda for boards? I, I think for directors to discharge their duties, they still need to be thinking about what is in the best interests of the company.

And we would expect them to be thinking about what information is relevant for their investors. So I guess our view is that by, by remove, by changing the thresholds and removing this very prescriptive framework with the associated li- liability, that you'll still receive rich disclosures around climate risks and opportunities but that there won't be... those efforts can actually be more focused on the quality of the information, um, in thinking about actual adaption strategies rather than spending a lot of time thinking about legal risks and assurance, um, provision.

Okay. Okay. I'm gonna go to our Deputy Chair, Ryan Hamilton. Um, thanks, Chair. And I guess just sort of supplementary to that, um, in terms of incentives, um, um... Oh, just had a question there, and it's gone. But it was more around, by adjusting the settings... Oh, you touched about in your submission, but could you expand on some things a little bit more? Could you touch on those things?

Do you mean in terms of bringing some of the exemptions into the legislation? Yeah. The amendment, you said you support it, but we could go a little bit further. Yes. Can So there's some quite technical amendments that the FMA has granted as class exemptions. Um, they relate to including a reference in your annual report to where your climate statements are available, and, um, that exemption, the result of that is that it effectively allows, um, climate-reporting entities more time to report. So we think that that would be a useful thing to bring

into the legislation. Um, there's a s- also an exemption of thinking about foreign issuers and, and the reporting obligations. And at the moment, they need to report if their, um, business presence in New Zealand is large. And again, we think that sort of an exemption, and the FMA is proposing to actually think about potentially excluding Australian issuers from the regime- Right. ... are useful things that could be brought into the legislation.

And that's because without it sitting in legislation, the FMA needs to go through a class exemption process for new exemptions. It creates some burden, I guess, on industry to be submitting into that process and, and is sort of somewhat, uh, inefficient. So we can tidy that up a bit. Yeah. For, for example, if they're already listed in Australia, why do they need to report twice, both- Right.

... in Australia and New Zealand, if they've already gone through a verification process? Mm-hmm. Okay. Very good. Todd Stephenson? Thank you, and thank you for being here. I mean, you briefly outlined, um, yeah, why some companies may choose not to list or delist. But can you also, 'cause since you're from the NZX, just outline how competitive is it to get investors, uh, to invest in companies, not just, well, obviously in New Zealand, but obviously people can take their capital and their money, investment money anywhere in the world.

Like, how competitive is the landscape we're talking about? Well, capital always flows to where it's most efficient, so it's trying to balance the efficiencies of the market with the right settings, and that's what we're hoping to achieve by supporting this amendment. And there's been a lot of significant reforms this year, including, um, the removal of the requirement for prospective financial information, which I guess is we're kind of thinking about here, the removal of the, um, unsubstantiated representation liability. So w- we're seeing good

regulatory settings that should foster that- that flow of money to New Zealand and New Zealand companies. Thank you. Okay, Kate, did you have one, uh, short question at any rate? Uh, very short then. Um, can you help the committee, um, with some of those changes which would make us... W- which would put us better on par with Australian issuers and to relieve some of the admin burden for Australian companies with a presence here, but at the same time making sure that we've got similar settings, um, so that, you know, those two

regimes talk to each other? Do you mean more broadly or in relation to climate? W- if we are as a committee to consider the amendments that you've put to us, which we will do, we haven't had submissions on those, so it's useful if you can kind of provide us with a bit more, um, context around the other things that we need to seek advice on.

Um, what I would say is these proposed changes do align us more with Australia in- in the shorter term. Um, the other thing that NZX has, uh, has been pushing for, because we look after the listed market, is also consideration of the unlisted market so it's a level playing field for- for climate reporting longer term, so it's not just the listed market that has to report.

Okay, right. We'll- we'll have to leave it there. Thank you so much for appearing before us, uh, today- Thank you. ... and for your supplementary, um, submission to, um, Kristin and to Simon. Simon being the general manager of corporate affairs and sustainability. It's quite a lofty title he carries these days, so thank you. Balancing act. (laughs) (laughs) Thank you, thank you. (laughs) Thanks, Simon.

Are we still friends? Are we still friends? (laughs) Probably not. Simon will say we never were. Um- Okay, Responsible Investment Association of Australasia. Um, got Dean online? Yes. Okay, Dean, let's go. Thank you for your patience. We look forward to it.

No, look, thank you, and, uh, thank you for the opportunity to contribute, uh, by today but also through, uh, what has been a- a- a really good and positive submission process. Uh, so, uh, ko Dean Hinga te toku ingoa. I'm the co-CEO of the Responsible Investment Association of Australasia. I'm the New Zealand-based co-CEO. We're the industry body for responsible investment.

We have, uh, a global investor base, uh, who are our member base, uh, who manage over $80 trillion in assets globally. Uh, within the Australia market we have all the major retail super funds and asset managers. Excuse me. Uh, and in New Zealand's, uh, 92% of the KiwiSaver market as well as all of the major, uh, private wealth, uh, providers, and so the vast majority of assets under management were within New Zealand. Uh, you've read the submission.

I know you've already heard from a number of other industry bodies, uh, many of whom share similar concerns to some of these changes, uh, that we do. Uh, so I wanted to start today just by being quite clear that, uh, the current regime is far from perfect. I think there's... We'd all acknowledge that there's lots of opportunity to improve outcomes, to reduce the burdens that are on business, while hopefully maintaining the majority of the benefits that the reporting actually gives us. And I know there's been a huge amount of focus on costs, but very little,

uh, or not as much as we believe is... uh, should be on the, uh, the actual benefits that come from this. So, uh, I know there's- there's already a lot of work that's actually been done through the XRB and, uh, around the potential of, uh, differential frameworks, uh, I'm sorry, differential reporting frameworks, which actually would meet a lot of the challenges that a lot of the organizations are facing.

And our concern is not so much that there are changes, uh, but it's the dramatic scale of the changes, uh, which actually really exceeds what was even consulted on initially. So three key things I'll highlight very quickly, uh, and I'm more than happy to take questions. Uh, the one, and- and I guess the- the biggest one I'd highlight is that we are living in a global capital market, uh, and we are a country that unfortunately does not dictate terms to that global capital market. And this is a move that is just completely out of step

with global developments. Climate risk, uh, the subsequent investment risk that it represents, it's becoming better understood every day. Uh, the impacts of it is becoming more pronounced every day. Uh, and the increased reporting required to help assess and price the risk, uh, is something that is only growing.

So by increasing these thresholds so significantly, we're reducing New Zealand companies' capability to understand and to communicate that risk. Right? Global investors need this information so that they can access any risk when they're investing in New Zealand and/or in- investing, uh, in companies within the country. I think that's really important as well.

Um, there's the investment they make within the reporting entities, but also broader investment that goes, uh, within New Zealand. Um, and whether they're big, uh, or it's specific projects, information from smaller businesses is actually necessary to determine what their risk is. Inconsistent reporting leads to unreliable and incomparable data. That's where we came from, and that is where we're gonna go back to, uh, if these thresholds are maintained. S- what that means is that the opportunity for investment is lost, uh, and we are increasing

inefficiency that is associated with it. Also want to be really clear about the signal that this sends to global investors. Right? Um, you know, there's already been lots of discussion about the, you know, the competitive nature of capital. There is simply no shortage, uh, of places for what is a huge amount of capital that's looking for a home to go. Right?

Um, we are in a scenario where global policy is moving so quickly, and New Zealand was seen as a leader in this space. It's an area where if you're standing still, you are moving backwards, uh, and if you are actively moving backwards as we are, uh, it really puts us, and takes us from being in a leading position, uh, into laggard position really, really quickly.Second thing I'll, uh, comment. Uh, w— totally agree on the importance of aligning with Australia, uh, completely disagree that this is in any way making us more

aligned to Australia. Uh, and there are several aspects that actually make it, uh, w- will ensure that we are less aligned with Australia, um, and it will take us further out of step with their policy. They're currently implementing this. Uh, that implementation is based on financial reporting thresholds which are based on revenue and assets. That's now very much being the global norm.

Um, we continue to use market cap which doesn't reflect the size and significance of entities on the economy, and I think frankly the idea of sort of trying to match thresholds dollar-for-dollar with an economy that is seven times the size of ours, uh, removes any concept of proportionate risk, uh, and I think it's a very long way from common sense. And I think if you look at, uh, the pension market, you know, the KiwiSaver, uh, balance on a good day is about $120 billion, right? In Australia, the only pension scheme is over $4

trillion, uh, and growing at a faster rate than ours. And so the idea that we match thresholds dollar-for-dollar does not make sense. Uh, and finally, yeah, just speaking to the mis-, you know, management removal, um, again, completely out of alignment with the Australian market. Uh, it means that our legislation will now be totally different. Uh, I think it's really, uh, the idea of differentiated reporting, as I mentioned earlier, uh, is a really valid one.

Um, but to completely remove it just weakens market transparency, it takes information away from consumers, uh, it's, it's completely out of alignment with even what the KiwiSaver market was asking for. And I think we've got to be really clear that, um, yes, there are some small entities who it w- did place a large burden on, um, but there are some huge entities dealing, who are managing $20, $30, $40 billion of New Zealand's capital, uh, and I think the suggestion that they're too small to be, uh, doing climate reporting just doesn't stack up.

And what would be far more beneficial is to take the learnings from the first round and improve and iterate it to make sure that, uh, we're making better use of the time and energy that's already been used. Okay, thank you, Dean. I'm gonna go to, um, Dr. Megan Woods, and then I'm gonna go to Julie Anne Genter. (clears throat) Hi, Dean. Good to see you, and thank you for the submission. (clears throat) Now, I know your organization has done a huge amount of work in terms of tracking the, the, the (clears throat) , the benefits and the

implementation of this regime. I'm really interested in you saying the regime's not perfect, there are changes we could make, um, and I think any scheme is always gonna have that. Um, so if you had to pick three things that we could improve about the current scheme, what would they be?

So, uh, I wouldn't even need three.
